Home ›Symbols ›Heart

Heart Symbol — Meaning & Symbolism

The heart is one of the most over-used and under-understood symbols in the world. It carries love, yes — but also courage, judgement, life-force, and the strange medieval geometry of an icon that looks nothing like the organ it claims to represent. To read it well is to recover what centuries of greeting cards have flattened.

The core reading: the seat of what cannot be spoken

Across nearly every tradition that has produced written reflection on the body, the heart is treated as the centre of something the mind alone cannot reach. It is the organ to which feeling is attributed, but more than feeling — it is the place where conviction, courage, conscience, and love are understood to live, often in tension with the head. The split between heart and mind is itself a symbolic claim about how human beings are constructed, and it shows up in cultures that had no contact with one another.

The stylised heart icon — the two-lobed shape we recognise instantly — is medieval in its present form but ancient in its impulse. It is a symbol that has been pressed into so many uses (Valentine's cards, emoji, brand logos, religious iconography, anatomical diagrams) that its original gravity is easy to miss. Read with care, the heart is most consistently a symbol of what is most vulnerable, most alive, and most worth protecting in a human being.

The most stable reading across traditions is this: the heart is the symbolic location of life-force and emotional truth. Whatever else it carries — love, courage, devotion, sorrow — those secondary meanings tend to flow from this foundational sense of the heart as the place where a person is most themselves, where pretence falls away, and where the consequences of how one has lived show first.

The heart across traditions

In ancient Egyptian belief, the heart (ib) was so central that it was left in the body during mummification while other organs were removed. The famous weighing of the heart in the Hall of Ma'at measured it against the feather of truth — too heavy with wrongdoing, and the soul was devoured. The heart here is not merely emotional but moral, the record-keeper of a life.

Aztec cosmology took the heart's centrality in a very different direction. The tonalli and teyolia life-forces resided in the heart, and ritual offerings of human hearts to Huitzilopochtli were understood as the fuel that kept the sun rising. Whatever modern revulsion that practice provokes, the underlying symbolic claim is striking: the heart contains the energy that sustains cosmic order.

Christian tradition, particularly through the late-medieval devotion to the Sacred Heart of Jesus, fused the heart with divine love, sacrificial wounding, and the inexhaustible source of mercy. The pierced and flaming heart became one of Catholic iconography's most enduring images. In parallel, the Sufi tradition speaks of the qalb — the heart as the organ of spiritual perception, capable of knowing God in ways the intellect cannot.

Chinese medicine and Daoist thought place the shen — the spirit-mind — in the heart, which is considered the emperor of the organ system. To disturb the heart is to disturb consciousness itself. Hindu traditions locate the anahata chakra at the heart, the centre of compassion and the meeting-point of upward and downward energies. Across Norse, Greek, and Celtic literature, the heart is consistently the seat of courage — to 'have heart' was to be willing to face what must be faced.

The icon itself — the symmetrical two-lobed shape — has murkier origins. Some historians trace it to the seed-pod of silphium, a now-extinct plant used as contraceptive in the ancient Mediterranean, which appeared on coins of Cyrene. Others point to ivy leaves, which carried associations with fidelity and were used in Greek and Roman decoration. By the fourteenth century the shape was stable in European art, and by the time playing cards spread across Europe it had become unambiguous.

The heart in depth psychology

Jung wrote less about the heart specifically than about the Self — the wholeness toward which the psyche moves — but the heart frequently appears in the dreams and active-imagination material of analysands as a symbol of that integrating centre. When the heart appears wounded, walled, or hidden in dream material, it tends to mark the relationship between the conscious ego and the deeper affective life it has been trying to manage from a distance. The work, in such cases, is rarely to fix the heart but to listen to what it has been trying to say despite being unheard.

Variations

The flaming heart. Often read as devotion, passion, or zeal — the heart not merely feeling but burning with a single-pointed orientation. In Christian iconography it marks divine love; in secular use it tends to signal intensity of commitment.

The broken heart. The most familiar variation, usually read as grief or loss, but worth distinguishing from mere sadness — a broken heart is one that loved enough to be broken, and many traditions read the crack as the place through which something larger can enter.

The pierced heart. Frequently associated with the Sacred Heart and with the Sorrowful Heart of Mary, but also with Cupid's arrow. The reading varies with context — sacred wounding versus the sudden, involuntary onset of love.

The locked or caged heart. Often interpreted as guardedness, sometimes as protection that has outlived its purpose. The question the symbol tends to pose is whether the lock is still serving the heart or simply isolating it.

The bleeding heart. Carries both the devotional reading of sacrificial love and the colloquial sense of someone whose compassion runs ahead of their judgement. Context decides whether this is honour or warning.

The glowing or radiant heart. Read across traditions as the heart in its full functioning — open, illuminated, generative. In yogic frameworks this corresponds to an unobstructed anahata; in Christian mysticism, to a heart in communion.

The two intertwined hearts. A specifically relational symbol — union, but also the entanglement of two emotional centres, which is not always read as wholly positive. Some traditions distinguish union from fusion, where the latter erases necessary individuality.

The heart with wings. Common in folk art and tattoo traditions, often read as love freed from earthly weight, or as the heart's longing to transcend. Can also signal love that has departed.

The anatomical heart. Increasingly used in contemporary art and tattoo work as a deliberate corrective to the stylised icon — a reminder that real love and real courage involve a real, fragile, muscular organ that can stop.

The shadow side: when 'follow your heart' becomes an evasion

The heart's enormous symbolic prestige has a cost. 'Follow your heart' is sometimes used to dignify decisions that the speaker has not actually thought through, or to override responsibilities that deserve weight. The heart, treated as an infallible oracle, becomes a way of refusing to consult anything else — including the people one's choices affect. Traditions that revered the heart almost universally paired it with discernment, conscience, or weighing; the modern habit of consulting feeling in isolation is a thinner inheritance than it pretends to be.

There is also the sentimental flattening — the heart reduced to an emoji or a Valentine's commodity, drained of the gravity that older traditions gave it. When everything is hearts, nothing is. The symbol works best when used with some restraint, and read with attention to which heart is meant: the loving one, the courageous one, the judging one, or the one that simply hurts.

A reflective practice

The next time the heart symbol surfaces meaningfully — in a dream, in art that stops you, in a phrase you used without thinking:

  1. Notice which heart is being invoked — affection, courage, conviction, grief, or life-force. The word covers too much to be useful undifferentiated.
  2. Ask what your heart, in that specific sense, has been trying to say that the rest of you has been managing not to hear.
  3. Rather than acting on the noticing immediately, sit with it long enough to know whether it is a passing weather pattern or something more settled. The heart deserves both honour and discernment.

Related interpretations

  • Rose symbolism — the rose and the heart share much of their iconographic history, particularly around love, devotion, and beautiful vulnerability.
  • Mirror symbolism — both heart and mirror are symbols of inner truth, what is revealed when pretence falls away.
  • Blood in dreams — closely linked to the heart's life-force register, often appearing alongside heart imagery in dream material.
